authoritative_sources.title: Business Guide to the FTC's Mail, Internet, or Telephone Order Merchandise Rule
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC is the primary U.S. consumer-protection regulator for remote-order handling. This guide is authoritative for delay notices, cancellation handling, prompt refunds, and order-record expectations when supplier failures affect pickup, delivery, or catering commitments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: order-delay, cancellation, and customer-commitment boundary review

authoritative_sources.title: Americans with Disabilities Act Title III Regulations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOJ's Title III regulations are the authoritative federal public-accommodation baseline for reservation handling in hospitality contexts. They are relevant when supplier disruption affects booked events or accessible hospitality commitments that cannot be improvised away.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: reservation, event, and hospitality-commitment impact review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Handbook 130 - Current Edition
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST Office of Weights and Measures publishes the public baseline used by many U.S. jurisdictions for posted-price and price-verification rules, making it authoritative when shortages or substitutions trigger menu-price or promotion disputes.
used_for: menu and POS price conflict handling
promotion or substitution pricing boundary awareness

authoritative_sources.title: FDA Food Code
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA's Food Code is the public food-service and retail-food safety baseline used by many jurisdictions. It is authoritative for approved-source, receiving, temperature, and record-sensitive boundaries that supplier coordination must not override.

authoritative_sources.title: Frequently Asked Questions: FSMA Food Traceability Rule
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA's current traceability FAQ is the authoritative public explanation of the FSMA traceability rule, its record expectations, and the current compliance extension. It is directly relevant when supplier coordination depends on lot-level or shipment-level traceability evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: traceability record review and evidence-pack assembly
used_for: note that compliance extension still leaves mixed tenant adoption and requires runtime retrieval

authoritative_sources.title: Fighting Identity Theft with the Red Flags Rule: A How-To Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC business guidance is the authoritative public baseline for recognizing suspicious contact, account, or payment-change activity that should move from routine coordination into fraud or loss-prevention handling.

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Personal Information: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC business guidance is an authoritative public reference for protecting sensitive guest, supplier, and payment-related information that can appear in supplier, booking, and refund cases.
used_for: limits on exposing guest or supplier personal data
safe packaging of sensitive case context

authoritative_sources.title: Standards in Use: Food Service
authoritative_sources.source_class: Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1 US is the recognized standards body for common supply-chain identifiers and traceability language. Its foodservice standards guidance is authoritative for vendor-neutral terminology and evidence structures used across suppliers, distributors, and operators.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: vendor-neutral terminology and handoff artifact normalization
used_for: GTIN, SSCC, ASN, and case-level traceability terminology

workflow_stages.name: Coordination intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize the request scope, location, supplier, order, receiving, event, and guest-impact context before any routing or closure output is produced.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references

workflow_stages.name: Supplier, receiving, and traceability dependency mapping
workflow_stages.description: Build the dependency chain across supplier confirmations, ASN, invoices, temperature logs, lot records, inventory state, and event commitments to determine whether the handoff can proceed safely.
source_classes: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: Cross-team handoff and ownership resolution
workflow_stages.description: Identify prior owner, next owner, destination acceptance, duplicate ownership, and required artifact completeness across purchasing, receiving, culinary, banquet, guest-services, pricing, fraud, and operations teams.
source_classes: Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: Customer-impact, refund, and policy-aware escalation
workflow_stages.description: Move shortages, substitutions, delay impacts, booking conflicts, refund pressure, and guest-service exceptions into explicit escalation states rather than silently forcing an answer this lane does not own.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references

workflow_stages.name: Safety, fraud, and sensitive-record stop-condition handling
workflow_stages.description: Preserve approved-source gaps, temperature abuse, traceability gaps, suspicious invoice or payment changes, and sensitive-record boundaries whenever supplier work carries safety or loss-prevention implications.
source_classes: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

workflow_stages.name: Closure confirmation and tenant adaptation control
workflow_stages.description: Issue closure only when destination owner, blocker resolution, and required evidence are explicit and any stricter tenant or jurisdiction rules have been surfaced rather than guessed.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

artifacts: supplier orders, acknowledgements, advance ship notices, invoices, and credit memos
receiving logs, temperature logs, approved-source records, lot or date-code records, shellfish tags, and hold notices
inventory states, par levels, menu availability states, 86 lists, commissary transfers, and shortage trackers
banquet event orders, catering commitments, reservations, service tickets, CRM or guest notes, and service-recovery requests
POS item status, booking records, order-management records, refund requests, comp notes, and promotion records
fraud-review notes, supplier master-data changes, sensitive-record access notes, and loss-prevention artifacts

allowed: Classify supplier coordination requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Track dependencies, duplicated ownership, and blocker state across supplier, receiving, culinary, booking, and guest-impact systems.
Produce handoff summaries, dependency trackers, routing confirmations, and closure confirmations when evidence is complete.
escalate: Required supplier, receiving, booking, payment, or inventory records are missing or materially conflict.
The request requires pricing override, refund approval, culinary substitution approval, food-safety disposition, fraud decisioning, or payment approval.
A public baseline and tenant, jurisdiction, brand, or contract policy materially conflict.
An approved-source gap, temperature excursion, traceability gap, invoice anomaly, or customer-harm issue changes whether the work may proceed.
refuse: Approve supplier orders, substitutions, pricing changes, refunds, or comps without explicit authority.
Authorize use of product with approved-source, temperature, traceability, allergen, or other food-safety uncertainty.
Adjudicate fraud or approve payment on suspicious supplier or invoice changes.
Close a case without destination-owner acceptance and required artifact completeness.

domain_failure_modes: supplier work is handed off without supplier confirmation, receiving ETA, or destination-owner acceptance and the event or service window is missed
purchasing, receiving, and banquet teams each assume another owner is handling the same shortage so the dependency is dropped
a short ship that affects a prepaid order is converted into a generic coordination note and the lane implies a refund or pricing decision it does not own
temperature-abused or approved-source-questionable product is routed toward use because service pressure overwhelms safety boundaries
traceability, lot, or shellfish records are treated as optional and the lane confirms closure without the required evidence chain
a suspicious bank-change or duplicate invoice is treated as ordinary vendor maintenance and routed without fraud escalation
tenant-specific supplier approval rules or event ownership maps are missing, and the lane guesses the next owner anyway
food-service terminology such as par levels, 86 list, BEO, or credit memo is flattened into generic vendor language and downstream operators lose context

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Refund or policy-exception request outside lane authority
Customer-impact issue with safety or fraud implications
Work is handed off without required context or artifacts
Multiple teams appear to own the same step
The lane is asked to absorb execution work instead of coordinating it
Ownership is ambiguous across multiple teams
A handoff is attempted without the required context

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Local health-department adoption of FDA Food Code details, tenant-specific approved-supplier rules, and brand-specific event ownership maps can materially change the next owner and required artifact set.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack uses FTC, DOJ ADA, NIST, FDA, and GS1 US sources as the public baseline. Runtime must still retrieve jurisdiction, brand SOPs, supplier approval rules, refund limits, and event or banquet ownership maps before tenant-specific outcomes are applied.

notes: The research baseline intentionally covers workflow, evidence, terminology, customer-impact handling, and failure behavior rather than generic vendor-management language.

FDA's traceability FAQ currently reflects a compliance extension, so the pack treats traceability expectations as a public baseline while still requiring tenant retrieval for exact current obligations and system hooks.

The lane stops at coordination. Procurement approvals, menu substitutions, pricing or refund decisions, fraud findings, and food-safety dispositions remain outside the lane even when service pressure is high.
